Week of 1/5 - 1/16 (26)

Weekly Market Summary

By Noah Halfon

Global Weekly Market Movements

U.S. Equities

  • S&P 500 Index: 6,940.01 (-0.06%)
    • SPX ends the week barely changed following Federal Reserve attacks, proposed policy changes, and geopolitical tensions. Gains led from XLP consumer staples up 5.01% and XLU utilities up 3.86%, with major movers BG and VST. Losses came mainly from XLF financials (-2.74%) after low Q4 earnings reports.
  • NASDAQ Composite: 23,515.39 (-0.26%)
  • Dow Jones Industrial Average: 49,359.33 (-0.23%)
  • Russell 3000 Index: 3,954.54 (0.13%)

Big Movers of the Week

    • Moderna Inc (MRNA): 41.84 (21.58%)
      • Moderna shares rose after the company announced preliminary sales metrics for FY25 at JPM Healthcare conference. MRNA reports revenue expectations towards the high end of guidance at $1.9B with a net cash of $8.1B, while also cutting $200M in opEX. MRNA also has a phase three melanoma trial that could end up being a key catalyst in 2026 upon positive results.
    • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD): 231.83 (15.28%)
      • AMD shares rose following broader semiconductor sector strength as TSM reported extremely strong Q425 earnings. Wells Fargo also upgraded AMD with a 55% upside for the end of the year on continued CPU leadership.
    • Intuit (INTU): 545.29 (-14.67%)
      • Intuit shares fell 6% on Wednesday after receiving multiple downgrades and slashed price targets. After reporting strong quarterly 2025 earnings with net profit doubling, analysts warn that it will be hard to follow. Broader software continues to get hit despite strong cash flow from fears of AI commoditizing workflows.

Chinese Equities - Shanghai Composite (SHCOMP): 4,101.91 (-0.80%)

Hong Kong Equities - Hang Seng Index (HSI): 26,844.96 (1.77%)

Japanese Equities- Nikkei 225 (NI225): 53,936.17 (5.00%)

European Equities

  • UK Index (UKX): 10,235.29 (1.09%)
  • German Index (DAX): 25,297.13 (0.19%)

Commodities

  • Gold Futures: 4,601.10 (0.47%)
    • Gold hits a new high above 4,600 an ounce due to Trump's probe into Fed independence and Iranian protests sparking investor demands for safe haven assets.
  • Silver Futures: 89.94 (7.78%)
    • Silver continues to outpace all other metals as precious metals rise amid geopolitical tensions. Silver climbed almost 8% to a new record of over $85 a troy ounce this week.

  • Crude Oil Futures: 59.22 (0.24%)

Crypto

  • Bitcoin (BTC): 95,362.00 (4.57%)
  • Ethereum (ETH): 3,322.56 (7.46%)

U.S. Monetary & Fiscal Policy

Fed Reserve Attack: President Donald Trump has opened a criminal investigation into Chair Jerome Powell related to Federal Reserve renovations. Markets reacted with notable calm, suggesting investor skepticism that the Fed’s independence is truly at risk, while numerous Central Bank heads rallied online to defend Fed independence. Still, rising political tensions reinforces a broader 2026 theme of diversification as investors look to overseas assets and trades. Gold and silver jumped as investors leaned into the “debasement trade,” signaling incremental shifts out of the dollar as USD index was down the most in 3 weeks to 98.75 on monday.

Global Macroeconomic News

Trade News: U.S. - Trump lowers tariffs on Taiwan to 15% as Taiwan pledges $250B in chip related investments. In other news, Trump threatens 25% tariff on countries doing business with Iran, and imposes a 10% tariff on Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the UK, Netherlands, and Finland effective February 1st. The tariff will increase to 25% beginning on June 1st unless the US reaches a deal to buy Greenland. //Greenland is heavy in rare earths which could push reliance towards China, ie. boosting US rare earth and defense names. Markets may not love this news, so opportunies to buy may arise.

Potentially Impacted Equities: AMKR, AMD, TSM, INTC, CRML, MP

Canada/China - Canada reaches a trade deal with China to lower trade barriers including tariff reductions in oil and Chinese EVs. They also plan to expand on energy deals (clean energy, nuclear, oil) as countries continue to try and diversify their export markets amid US tariff attacks.

BoJ/JPY: Japan’s “Takaichi trade” is back in focus after PM Sanae Takaichi signaled plans for a snap election. Known for her dovish monetary policy and aggressive fiscal spending, Japanese equities surged with the Nikkei 225 jumping 5% to new records. A Takaichi-led majority in the lower house would likely continue equity strength but pressure the JPY as fiscal deficits expand and rates hikes slow. The Yen slid to its weakest level since July 2024, while long dated JGB yields like the 30 year rose 12 bp to 3.6% for the week ended. The BoJ continues to grow more sensitive to Yen related inflation risks, with possible further rate cuts after next week’s policy decision.

Unemployment: The US added 50,000 jobs in December (+37K private sector jobs, and +13K government jobs) ending out 2025 with the weakest job growth since 2003 with just 584,000 total jobs added. Despite earlier rate cuts to support a struggling labor market, December’s unemployment rate dropped from 4.5% to 4.4%. Suggesting that the labor market is not drastically worsening, CME Fedwatch saw rate cut probabilities for January drop from 15% to 2%. The next projected reduction remains in mid-2026.

CPI and Core CPI: Underlying US inflation rose in December by less than expected as Core CPI ticks up from 2.6% YoY 2.7% YoY. Core CPI MoM increased 0.2% VS 0.3% expectations. Shelter costs were the largest contributor in the monthly advance, rising 0.4% in December, while airfares and grocery prices also jumped. Vehicle repair costs fell by the most on record.

Bank Earnings / Financial Sector:

JPMorgan - Stocks slid as investment banking fees missed guidance, with IB revenue falling 5% YoY to $2.35bn amid weaker underwriting and M&A advisory activity. With a strong macro backdrop for the sector, JPM still delivered $46.8bn in quarterly revenue with total client asest up 20% YoY to $7.1 trillion with $25bn in net interest income.

Morgan Stanley - Fixed income underwriting division reported revenue of $785M, 93% increase from last years quarter. Driven by major AI spend race and low rates, many mega tech companies may be using this opportunity to finance cheaply from debt markets rather than FCF spend (ex. Meta raising $30B in corporate bonds in October 2025, Alphabet $25B, Amazon $15B).

Blackrock - Reports record $14 trillion AUM with a 25% YoY increase in revenue to $7.0 B. EPS beat also came in at $13.16 EPS vs $12.2 expected with $267.8 B quarterly net inflows. Performance fees jumped 67% YoY, while the quarterly dividend was raised 10%.

Wells Fargo - Reported $5.4bn in net income a 13% gain YoY and returned $23bn to shareholders through dividends and buybacks, benefiting from the removal of its Fed asset cap.

Bank of America - Saw revenue rise 7% to over $28bn and net income jump 18% to $7.6bn driven by solid loan growth and a 23% surge in equities trading. Net interest income also grew 10%, a major driver of the quarter.

Takeaways: Overall higher expense guidance, partly driven by investments in tech, but also from concerns around the proposed 10% credit‑card interest‑rate cap (average 24%). Lower rates could mean lower revenue and FCF projections, but also heightened risks for lower income brackets where rates are currently inherently lower and lending money is risksier. Revenue growth remains strong across the sector, along with continued financial deregulation and positive interest rate outlooks from the Fed.

Sources: Google Finance, Market Watch, CME Fedwatch, Yahoo Finance, Reuters, New York Times, Bloomberg, Wall St Journal, Washington Post, US Department of the Treasury, GuruFocus, Edward Jones

Noah Halfon
